Welcome everybody. We're going to have some fun today. We're going to look at three promotions that have been submitted by our members. Those promotions are going to be in three different areas actually. There's one health promotion. There's one promotion for an investment newsletter. There's a third promotion that's for opportunity seekers that has to do with starting and operating a business on eBay. Let's get going. The first promotion we have is by one of our members. Her name is Belinda Brewster and she's arguably one of the very best investment newsletter promotion copywriters in the business today. I just love Belinda. She's got a great spirit and a great smile. She does a fantastic job on her copy. I understand that part of the creative team on this also included some other people at Agora that I respect very highly. The reason that I mention that is because in my critique I'm going to talk about some mistakes that I believe that were made in the very early stages of creating this promotion. The reason that I feel fairly free naming Belinda and the people at Agora is that I've made the same mistake not once but several times over my career. This is a really good morality lesson I think for all of us and it will help us all in all of our promotions. The first thing that I noticed when I looked at this promotion was the headline. The seven shocking lies, how Washington Weasels, Wall Street Huxters, media lap dogs, betray you, cheat you, lie to you and plan to rob you blind. The shocking lies headline has been used an awful lot over the years. To me that in this case is almost a strike against it. Our market for financial newsletters has been shrinking over the years. The people who are still in that market who are still subscribing to newsletters are die hearts. There are core audience. I've said before that a few years ago there were 10 million prospects for this kind of a product. Today that number is probably shrunk in terms of direct mail. It's probably shrunk to about 1 million or maybe even less. We're talking to our real core audience, people to whom investing is a passion and who love getting investment advice for newsletters. That means by definition that they've seen this kind of headline seven shocking lies many, many times before. The second thing that I want to point out is how the benefit to reading this is buried The first thing I'm going to see because of the size of the type is the main headline in the deck. The benefit to reading this, the bribe, what you're going to give me if I give you my time and read this is fairly small type and easy to miss. One other thing and I'll return to this in a moment, but notice how this is all about how they are betraying me, cheating me, lying to me. As I was reading this I started getting the feeling that I was kind of a putz. I'm being taken advantage of because obviously they think I'm not very bright. These Washington Weasels, Wall Street Huxters, Media Laptops, Ducks. This is a minor point and probably could be handled very easily, but there's a principle here that it's very important to constantly be in touch with how your prospect is feeling as he's reading your copy and how does that copy reflect on him. If this deck for example, maybe took the approach that they don't think you're smart enough, they don't think you're good enough, they don't think you're disciplined enough, we know different. Here's how to out with them. That would be more on the prospect side. Minor point, I don't want to belabor it. Let's move on to the bottom of the first page. This is the outside of a 24-pager. Now we have also inside the Thieving Liars Club and then we have seven things here that they're lying to you about. The first one is deficits don't matter. The second one is your retirement is safe. The third one is inflation is under control. Next, the housing bust is over. The economy is strong. The U.S. is the richest country in the world and then finally this stock is hot, is supposedly a Wall Street broker who's saying that. What struck me here is that there's really no new news here. This is sending me the signal that you're going to be talking to me about stuff that I've read about in a lot of other promotions over the years and of course in the newsletters I take. Deficits don't matter. The old deficit argument is as old as the hills. Your retirement is safe. Social security is going to go bankrupt any minute now. They've been saying that for 40 years. Inflation is under control. We've all read the arguments that the Fed keeps a set of crooked books to report on inflation to make it appear smaller than it really is. These have been topics in promotions and in newsletters over the years many, many times. There's nothing new here so far. The housing bust is over. Maybe it has some potential but of course I'd shy away from that as a major topic because I've had some negative experience with that in the last year in the mail. Seems that the people we talk to don't really care that much about the housing bust at this point. The only people that really affects are real estate investors or at least that seems to be the perception in the market because if I own a home and it goes down 20% value that's not going to make me want to sell it. It won't have any effect on how I live my life today. The economy is strong. Seems kind of like an esoteric argument. It's like okay now we're going to sit down and we're just going to have it debated on whether the economy is strong, whatever that means or not. I don't feel compelled yet to dive into this. The US is the richest country in the world. Again, same thing. It signals to me that you're going to have a discussion and tell me the US is not the richest country in the world but why do I care? This isn't about me. This is about some statistic someplace. It doesn't really affect how rich I am or how rich I could get. And then finally this stock is hot about obviously brokers not telling me the truth. And of course we've been hitting on this for years. I want to say that I saw a strong influence in this opening copy especially from Gary Benzvang's Lies, Lies, Lies package. And I have to say throughout the copy is excellent. The copy writing is excellent. I think the strategic decision to go with a kind of a lies, lies, lies theme but then apply it to kind of esoteric arguments rather than things that really affect me. In the Benzvang package the lies, you know, there was a fat broker, there was a banker who was ripping you off, there was this guy, there was that guy. But each one of them was doing something to me personally. In this case these tend to be like academic arguments more or less. And I think that's an error. And it's an error that I get sucked into from time to time myself. I think in my entire career I've only resigned one assignment and it was for KCI and it was on the grain of the baby boomers. And this kind of ties into the social security thing on the cover. On the grain of the baby boomers I wrote this just really hot copy about how they're going to drive all these stocks up. But the package didn't work well. When we went back and looked at it and did our autopsy on it we decided that it was because we were talking delayed gratification. The threat and the benefit were too far off in the future to really make the readers say, hey, I've got to read this right now. And so you have delayed gratification. You have academic arguments that don't really affect the quality of life of the prospect right now today. And the benefits are buried there on the cover as I said as well. Also the solution, the solution which is buy these hot stocks doesn't solve the problem. The problem is I'm being played for a sucker. They're lying to me about all of these things. So to get even go out and buy these stocks and you'll get rich and living well as a best revenge kind of thing. But that's not really the solution to the problem is it? The problem is posed is that you're being played for a sucker. The solution then isn't to get rich. That would be a great lead for a promotion for example for a political organization that was raising funds to throw the guys out of office. Or it would be a great lead for a, well, I think that's the main application I can see for it. But if you're coming to me and you're saying that these guys are lying to you so buy these stocks, buy my newsletter and buy these stocks, there's a disconnect, a massive disconnect and it doesn't give me the emotional satisfaction or even direct satisfaction. It doesn't fix the problem in any way. So I hope I was clear on that. Okay, this is the top of the table of contents page. I don't have a lot to say here because basically we're kind of repeating, I think, we're launching off of the theme on the public, on the cover rather. So we have Washington's scary lives of omission, how they scam us. Okay, well, so politicians lie, okay, that's front page news that nobody ever noticed that before. And how they scam us by hiding trillion dollars debt off budget, this confirms my suspicion of this something I've read before. Heaven help us politicians set to fix our retirement, but is the cure worse? And not new news. You're being robbed, the sneaky little shell game the Washington plays to deflate the two shocking inflation numbers. I don't need to go through these again, but the point is that this is, it's not news and therefore there's no urgency to read this now. The opening copy is excellent. Blender's a great writer, there's a lot of energy. I just love the tone of this piece. And I was kind of wishing I had asked Blender to work with me on another promotion that I'm doing right now that does have a political leap, but it's not for a financial newsletter. It's for a newsletter that has more to do with lifestyle quality of life, managing your finances, insulating yourself from the blunders that Washington is about to make. But anyway, the copy is wonderful. You just notice the emotionally energized tone here. It's just too bad that it's being applied to a general theme that seems doomed from the outset. You know, I've talked about this before. When you're operating at Belinda's levels and Belinda writes a package or I write a package or Carline or Paris or any of these other top writers write a promotion for a financial newsletter, quite often, in fact, in most cases, the factor that will determine whether the package beats the competition, beats the control tends to be the theme, not the copy. You know you're going to get great copy. You know you're going to get just kick butt copy right down the line. The variable is will the theme resonate with the prospect or not? Is this something he's thinking about, frustrated about, and can you offer a solution that directly connects to that frustration? And in this case, I'm afraid the theme is the problem in this package. And that's a real problem because the copy follows the theme throughout as we'll see in a moment. It's really not fixable without rewriting about the first half of the package. Having said that, you know, it would take some time to do, but you could do it and save the package, but it would take a tremendous amount of time. I'm afraid. Having said all of that, let's skip on ahead to page four. See that's page one. This thing is lagging here a little bit. H2, H3, 2. I want to also mention, you know, I don't think the graphic artist helped you much with this in terms of promoting readership. This would just be, in so many of the sidebars, you have this very long line length with very tiny copy, but it would have been much better to have broken sidebars like this up into two or even three columns so you have the shorter line length and better readability. Also for contrast, it's a good idea quite often to vary the typeface. Here you're using a serif typeface down here. I think you could have used a fairly condensed sans serif face and gone to two or three columns and actually saved some space and then able to come up and use a larger typeface. Okay, let's see, three, four. All right, let me just, I think we found the Achilles' heel of the package, but let's talk about just a few other principles that I found when I was going through the package. We've kind of set the stage in the early copy. You can go back and read it again. I think she did a good job of kind of establishing the product and even the offer up front and expanding on the theme. Now here we are on page four and we begin with our Lies copy. My number one deficits don't matter. I just want to point something out here. The person who said that is Vice President Dick Cheney. I've got a real problem with this because I understand that the administration's popularity rating is probably only about 30% or probably less at this point. The prospect is probably thinking that the administration is a bunch of clowns in a big clown car and they're running around making buffoons of themselves. The problem is that your prospect, if you're writing to investors, is not the population at large. Your prospect is one of those 30% who think the administration is doing a great job. Your prospect is a conservative, most likely Republican, fairly substantial contingent of libertarians probably, very few Democrats and they're very conservative and they tend to support the President and the Vice President. Right here on page four we have Lies number one deficits don't matter and we immediately start taking a pot shot at Dick Cheney because Dick Cheney is the one who said deficits don't matter. Again, got to keep in mind who you're talking to and in this case you're talking to the 30% who think Dick is a good guy and you've just run the risk of alienating a substantial portion of them. Let's skip ahead to page eight. I think in the future I'm going to only have the pages. I'm going to actually talk about this thing as well as well. By the way everybody, next time we're going to be using a different conferencing company. I'm really excited about it and I think you'll like the interface better, the recording should be better and also the images. It's just better technology. Let's see here. Page eight. Notice here at the end of this line. This is line number two and at the end of this line we just go straight into line number three. One of the things that really works about this kind of crisis number one, crisis number two, line number one, line number two, opportunity number one, opportunity number two, kind of outline for a promotion is that you weave your premium and your offer into the copy. That helps keep people reading. It helps build value of the premium. At the end of line number two here we should have copy explaining how our premium, how our free gift that we've already billboarded is going to address this problem. Then maybe two or three fascinations from the premium. Then say it's a $79 value but I want to send it to you free. I'll tell you how to get it in a moment. The first let's look at line number three and then you move on. If you do that at the end of every line, the line section of this which is basically the heart of the front end of this package is doing more than just talking about the ways I'm being ripped off. It's actually selling me on that premium and why not getting it would be the dumbest thing I could possibly do. I just want to point out that in this case Belinda does come back to the line. On several occasions as she's going through this but it really needs to be a drumbeat, almost a mantra at the end of every line. This is how, in other words, the lie itself is the reason why you need the premium and why you might as well just slash your wrist and quit and die if you don't get it right away because it's the only thing that's going to save you. It needs to tie back directly. All right. Let's go up to page number 12. See if this thing can keep up with me. Okay. You'll notice here again, we're not on 12 yet, but you'll notice here again after line number six there's no connection to the premium. Now I have to say this was almost like a little stone in my shoe. All of the other lies had to do with Washington's Social Security, the budget, the deficit, the inflation rate, the housing bust and they weren't personalized very much. We did have the quote from Dick Cheney and so forth, but if you're going to lionize somebody, whether it's the Treasury Secretary or the Vice President or if you want to take your life in your own hands and go after the administration with this group, that's fine. I wouldn't do it, but whoever you're going to lionize, find outrageous photographs of them. I'm doing a package right now where I'm going after the other side of the aisle. I'm going after Pelosi and Hillary and Charlie Rangel and Kacinich and the Democrat side and some of the proposals that they have on the table right now that would absolutely kill the economy. I found the most ridiculous photographs of each one of them. People don't get emotionally exercised when they're thinking about Congress or government, but if you show them the picture of a politician who's trying to pick their pocket or restrict their freedoms or who's insulted them by saying they're greedy or something terrible about them or who's refused to fix Social Security and Medicare and then applauded when the President mentioned how his bill had been defeated, then all of a sudden you see these pictures of these people you already hate. In this market, Teddy Kennedy is like, they love to hate this guy. His picture is like gold. If you want to generate an emotional involvement, you show people like that to these guys and you're going to get a reaction. I just wanted to show you a little graphics, Boo Boo, here on page 12. I mentioned another one earlier. I think your graphic artist is really hurting you here. I have to spend a lot of money on graphics. I understand this is a gore and they really like to do their graphics on the cheat and they like them to look. They like their promotions to look like they were put together very quickly and so they're more topical and timely and not a lot of production value. That doesn't let you off the hook when it comes to making your piece readable. The human eye goes across a line of tiny type this long. By the time you get to here, or by the time you get all the way back over to here, you've forgotten what you read on the line earlier. The eye fatigue is quite easily. This is pretty much wasted space. I would doubt if one in 10 people even read this sidebar. From here on, though, the copy picks up because now we're going to be talking about the premium and the seven stocks in the premium that you're going to get. From here on out, this package is gold. Belinda did a great job. I really want to know about these companies and I want the premium. I had to wade through 12 pages of stuff that really didn't apply to me and that didn't connect with the solution. The principle is here, old news never sells. They've read it before. They get it. They understand it. In fact, at a cocktail party last night, they were probably lecturing somebody else on how the government cheats on its inflation numbers and so forth. Old news never sells. The head and deck copy, you've got to be careful not to imply that your prospect is a patsy because he's so easy to take advantage of. Number three problems you lead with must be completely resolved by your solution, both emotionally and practically. Number four, remember who you're writing to. Don't go after somebody that your prospect loves. Five, build desire for the main premium on every single page and weave that sell throughout. It's so easy when you're getting into the research on these kinds of things to kind of lose track of what you're really doing. The first half of this package was basically a sales pitch for a premium that they're going to get free. That's what it's there for. Everything that's supported that buying that premium should be there and everything that doesn't shouldn't. The point of pages one through 12 should be to make that premium seem like, man, if I don't get this, I'm in deep trouble. That's the key. I hope that helps, Belinda. I love you with all my heart. Please don't be mad at me because I made similar mistakes many, many times and not too long ago either. I do want to point out something here I thought was ingenious and I don't know whose idea this was, whether it came from Agora or Belinda, but this offer is just great. They're essentially saying that, look, you need this so badly that I'm willing to pay for your first year subscription myself. The best deal of the year is you get a full year free. You get your first year free and you're signing up for two years. I'll pay for your first year. Isn't that great? That's because I'm a good guy and because I'm absolutely convinced I can help you. I think that's a great way to go. Then they have a six-month free offer as well. This basically the entry-level membership. That's basically it. Go ahead and move on to the next promotion. This is for something. This promotion was actually mailed in the UK. It was created for the UK market. I thought this was really interesting too because I've taught these principles quite often, some of the principles you'll see in here. In this case, I believe they've been misapplied and I'll tell you why. First of all, if you look at the PDF that you have, you can see what this piece looked like when it came in the mail. My first response to the front was, what the heck is this? It's not a special report. It's not a magazine. What is it? It doesn't look like a magazine. The front cover, and I'll switch back and forth here in a minute, this is the top portion of the front cover. This is the bottom portion of the front cover. You can see it's very copy heavy, has little tiny photographs. That was my first reaction to it. People are comfortable getting information from things that look familiar. That's why maga logs work. Even why tabloids work. You won't find anything that looks like the tabloids we mail in a bookstore. You will see magazines, basically it's a magazine on steroids. It's a big magazine. They tend to look and feel like magazines. I'm not really sure how to get my arms around. I really don't know what it is. By the way, this is for a product called a continual sales system. That product name needs some serious help. I have no idea what a continual sales system is. Typically a product name should contain a benefit, or at the very least an intrigue element. Continual sales system sounds sterile, sounds black boxy, like some technological or technical thing that I don't really know how it works. I got to trust it, but it's going to somehow do something. I would suggest some serious consideration on a benefit-oriented product name. Okay, his lead is interesting. Obviously the cover is too copy heavy. I want to just point to this. I wear glasses. I had to put on two pair of glasses to read that little blurb. You can see more of it on this page. Look, it's just kind of nestled in here. I have it obviously on my screen right now. It's magnified substantially. I have no idea what's going on with that thing. Also look at the reverse copy here. You want to guarantee something won't be read, put it in 12 or 13 point text, and reverse it out of black. You can pretty much be sure no one's going to read it. That would be a real great way to present a disclaimer if you don't want people to read it. Again, that's just a graphic comment, but man, I tell you what. If people don't read it, they can't buy. Going back to some of the issues that we've written in the total package about graphics would do this person a lot of good, I think, the person who had this design and who wrote it. Want to point out the lead. When I first read this, think about what happens when you read a promotion. What's going on in your mind? What's going on in your mind is you're trying to predict where the writer is going to go next. As you're reading a paragraph, you're kind of guessing as you're moving along. Where is this going? What's this conclusion going to be? What's coming next? Because I'm reading this thing, that's what I'm doing. I'm going, okay, what's the product? I read the headline. It says, I've tried every money-making internet skin going and I never made a damn penny from any one of them. That's a skeptical lead. I'm not sure it's right for this market, though. I remember this is an internet profit product. This is an opportunity-seeker product. As we get into it, we'll see that he's going to show you how to make money on eBay. Who are opportunity-seekers? Opportunity-seekers are dreamers. They're perpetually optimistic. They have this vision, this dream for their life and they believe they can make it happen and they want to go out there and make it happen. Is a skeptical lead really the best way to approach them? It wouldn't have been my first choice. We'll talk a little bit about how I might have done it in a moment. I want to point out that on the cover we have some testimonials, which is always good. This does appeal to the entrepreneurial or opportunity-seeker portion of the audience. We have a pub note that begins here. It continues on the next page that essentially introduces the concept of the missing ingredient. As I was reading this now, I'm thinking ahead, okay, where's this going? What's the product? I came to the conclusion at this point that the product was something called the missing ingredient, the missing factor. You can see it right here. I'm going, okay, I've got to find out what the missing factor is. Or how, I think it's kind of getting an idea here. The missing factor is hardcore real-life proof from numerous people who've used select the information to really make money and change the lives in the process. So, okay, proof. Okay, that's the missing factor. Now I'm really confused because I'm thinking the promotion is going to tell me how to use proof to sell my products on the Internet. Okay? But that's not what it's saying at all. Because we'll see in a moment what it's saying is, look, all of our competitors promise to make you money. And they show you nice testimonials of people who say, I love the product, but they never give you testimonials that say how much an individual actually has made with the product in over what period of time. In other words, they're not specific testimonials. And this is the writer's way, I have to assume, of pointing out the fact that all the testimonials you're about to read are very specific. And they tell you exactly how much money I'm making. Each guy makes who's used my system. And they even give you the guy's full name and postal code. I mean, it's pretty amazing. I don't think I could get away with that over here. But so anyway, the lead really caught me off guard. And it had me totally confused. I just want to point out also that on this page, page two, we have kind of a table of contents here that lists some of the testimonials that you're about to read, some of the other material that's inside. Table of contents on page two is great. But remember, what you're trying to do is to bribe people to read this. So when you're doing your table of contents articles, you need to give a benefit. Why should I take this internet money making quiz? Finding out whether or not I'm the kind of person that could profit from this is not going to move me to read this. Finding me if I'm the next online bill gate or if I have what it takes and then offering some benefit, some other benefit to taking the quiz would have been a big help. This is pretty good. Quick and easy way to get a product to sell. And yes, it's proven. That kind of makes me want to read, especially if I'm an entrepreneur and I've considered starting my own online business. This device here is very good. I use it in a lot of my promotions. We're right up front, you tell them, in this case, 226 pounds, an internet money making tool for free. So that's a positive. Now we go on to the beginning of the running article and this is on page three. And once, you know, here's where we expand on the idea of the missing factor. I think we wasted the first four pages of this piece. I think the lead is really on page four and we'll get there in a moment. The message we're trying to send here could have been handled in one blurb of copy early on that just says, other people tell you their products great, you know, our people tell you they're making thousands, you know. And not in a year, in a week or in a month or even in a week. And for this market, I would have been tempted to go with something more like my EOC cover. Also, I want to say that, you know, on my Enhancer Oculation Package for Health Resources, I use the phrase real people, real profits, proof inside. And that communicated the whole thing and not profits, real people, real results, proof inside. And that communicated the uniqueness of my testimonials. My EOC cover was a picture of an older gentleman and he was proclaiming, my doctor says I have a new heart. And then that followed with that and that was like 72 point type. And then under it, it followed with nice 14 point follow up, the beginning of this guy's story, about how he was dying with heart failure until he discovered this product. In this case, we could have something like the amazing secret of push button profits as a prehead and then a picture of Amanda who will meet in a minute, Amanda O'Brien with 72 point type saying, I'm working two hours a day and making 7,000 pounds a month on eBay. I think, and then just go straight into the hard hitting and you can even open it with the statement, you know, other people tell you their product is great. Our people tell you how it feels to grow rich. And then just go into the sales copy that we'll look at beginning on page 4. I think burying a lead is one of the most common mistakes that writers make and I think in this case, remember the forehead slap test, okay? I don't think many people woke up in the middle of the night thinking, you know, and slapped themselves on the forehead and said, you know, oh my God, what, you know, I need to know what the missing factor is or oh my God, I need to know, I wish all these guys would add specifics to their testimonials, okay? But they're waking up thinking is, I don't have enough money to pay the bills or I really want that new car, that new house and I can't afford it and if somebody would just come along, I know it's not that hard, dumber people than me are getting rich. If someone would just come along and show me how to do this, you know, that would be so much to me, you know. So if you go in with that kind of a lead, you know, Amanda's lead saying, two hours a day I make it 7,000 pounds a month, I'm doing it on eBay, I think that was another thing that was missed here. Just to mention the word eBay is a big plus. It's an implied endorsement almost, you know. We haven't really called out our audience here, you know, who's our audience? Well, our audience is a bunch of people that want to get rich as quickly and easily as possible. They know what eBay is and they know that people are getting rich on eBay and I think it's on page four before we actually refer to eBay in the context of this product and I think that's a mistake. I would bring eBay right up front. As we'll see in a moment, we also need to bring the product into sharper focus and we'll talk about ways to do that. Also throughout this piece, I would go through and just focus on sidebars. I mean, just focus on subheads. The subhead should draw the eye down into the text. You know, we've talked about there are two kinds of readers out there. There are readers and there are scanners, two kinds of prospects, readers and scanners. A reader is a meticulous left-brain guy who'll sit down, start with page one and read every word of the running text and then he'll go back maybe and read the sidebars. But he's going to do it sequentially and he's going to do it the way he was taught in grade school. Then there's the scanner. It's on the scanner. He'll hold it in his left hand, start by looking at the back cover and then leave backwards through it just scanning the sidebars, you know, or the subheads. And so we've got every subhead, every sidebar is an eye magnet intended to drive the prospect down into the running text where the real selling is happening. And the subheads in here are really kind of misused and we'll take a look at that in a minute. Just so I won't forget, I have a note about this. Also, the testimonials need to be in the customer's voice. Yeah, the customer's voice. We'll see how that's not being done right now. And again, we have readability issues here where the graphics are hurting us some. So let's skip ahead to page four where the lead really appears in this piece. Let's see here. Okay. Here's your lead right here. The only way for the little guy to profit from today's burgeoning internet revolution. My name is Oliver Grohler. No joke here, but I seriously believe I've helped more people become eBay power sellers than anyone else in history. Okay. That's a really good lead. It's friendly. It's straightforward. There's absolutely no dilly-dallying around. I'm not going to waste your time. Look, you want to get rich. I know how to do it. Let's get started. You know, we have a control right now for WISE that Eric Betel wrote and the six giants of global profits is a headline. And the first line of running tax is it goes something like, I'm not going to waste your time. The countries I'm talking about are China, Australia, Canada, and Elyssa, six countries where the investor is going to find these profits. A fast start is essential. And so I would start here. I think it's a great place to start. However, on page five, okay, we have, on page five here, we have a testimonial, which is wonderful. However, look at this. Again, we've got those long lines of little, tiny text and it really hurts readership. At the bottom of the page, let's see, is it going to go? There it is. We begin our reasons why the prospects should be interested in eBay power selling or continual selling. Now, just for those of you who haven't read it, and I think I have this right, okay, continual selling is essentially, as I understand it. You go on eBay and there are these basically stores that are on there all the time. The listings never run out. And people aren't really bidding. There's just basically their prices for products. I don't know if they do just buy it now, I guess, and so their prices for products. So you throw up a store. It's up there forever. You just pay eBay for the privilege of having your store up there. So it's different from the bidding thing that I always do where I list a car and people come and bid on it. So that's basically it. And what we're going to do is we're going to show you how to use that new feature of eBay, the continual sale feature, to build a stream of income or multiple streams of income. Now, as you're looking at this in your own time, take a look at this area right in here because this is a place to really present the benefits of the system as a stream of bullets. There's that approach that I talk about all the time, which is tell them what you're going to tell them, then tell them, and then tell them what you told them. Well, this would be a really good place to tell them what you're going to tell them. You don't have to get into detail. It could be a string of one-line bullets, but it can give me the reasons why I need to keep reading this and the reasons why you're going to bring tremendous value to my life with your product. And this might even be a good... If you're going to introduce a product to me, you've already done it. You've talked about the continual sale system approach over here. So this would be a great place to present your first photograph of the product. And we'll talk more about that in a moment because there are ways to do that that will really help, I think. Now, remember when I was saying that the graphics didn't help much on this? Look at how long this paragraph is. Well, let's read through it real quick. You need huge markets on the web, and this is what you'll get by selling products on continual sale in the correct markets on eBay. I recently calculated... Oh, that would be a perfect place to break this paragraph into two paragraphs, wouldn't it? I recently calculated that some markets as much as 50,000 pounds of product is sold on continual sale per day, and that's every day. Wow, there's another great place to break this paragraph. Take a slice of that action for yourself. Wow, you could break it again here. Fact is, with the continual sale, that way, take a slice of that action for yourself could be on a line... two lines by itself and really draw attention to itself, wouldn't it? So, okay, let's break it here again. Start a new paragraph. Fact is, you see, you could broken this into five or six paragraphs, made the page look less intimidating, brought more emphasis to the most important points of your argument. Also, in this area here, this is where you need to differentiate very clearly. Actually, let's jump back a little bit over in here. Or maybe even over in here. You need to differentiate more clearly between normal eBay selling and this continual sale system. Because, you know, I read this several times and I still am not totally clear what continual selling is on eBay. So, I would suggest that you really take a look at that and see what you can do to clearly differentiate the old way versus the new way. The old-fashioned way that eBay is to do it this way, this new way is explosively more profitable. It's easier. You do it once. It makes you money for a lifetime and so forth. All right, so let's move over to six. All right, I think it's at the bottom of the page you wanted to call your attention to something here. Oh, I know what it was. Okay, so we have our reasons. The first one was that you could, what was it? Huge markets, right? Reason number two, you can start multiple businesses. You're not just confined to doing this once. You could start a new business every day if you wanted to using the system. Reason number three is that there's zero customer contact. For anyone who's intimidated by the act of selling or doesn't want to put up with customer complaints, that's a positive. And then you have no selling to do. Again, wow, I don't like selling, so this is really good for me, right? But I really think that the last two reasons there are kind of weak. And maybe probably should be combined maybe into one at the very most if you want to keep them. There are some other reasons I think that occurred to me that would be more powerful. The first one is that I can reach millions of prospects for next to nothing. If I'm going to do direct mail, it's going to cost me 60 cents to contact one prospect. And for X dollars, I can have thousands, tens of thousands, even hundreds of thousands visiting my online store. So my cost of selling is negligible. I think that's a big plus. Another one is that thousands of great name brand products are available for a song, and the wholesaler even ships the product for you. I think more could have been done with that. There are two elements to this whole business model. The first is knowing how to list and sell the product. But you know how many people are out there right now that just have no idea what to sell? And so showing them what is selling and giving them the sources, the places they can go to get products at wholesale and even have the wholesaler ship for them would be a big plus. And then the final point should really be the kind of the crescendo, the culmination of these points. And to me, it's that once you post, once you have your online store up there, all you have to do is sit back and watch the money roll in. eBay or PayPal collects the money for you. Basically, your job is to spend it. Now you're getting back in touch with why this person is interested in the opportunity seeker kind of thing in the first place. John asks, what's the maximum number of lines to have in a three column paragraph or a two column paragraph? I don't like rules. I just like, as I'm reading through the copy, first of all, at the very least to make sure that each paragraph is basically communicates a single thought. That's just basic grammar, right? But if I can find other ways to break it up and have shorter paragraphs, I will. I love one paragraph, or one line paragraph, regardless of the number of columns on the page. The old rule is five lines to a paragraph. But that applied mostly to sales letters where you have full page width text. So in columns, you're never going to achieve that ideal. And if you look at any of the promotions I've done over the years, you'll see that there are some paragraphs in there that are longer than they should be. But the goal is that you want to keep those paragraphs close fairly short. And because for one thing, every time you break a paragraph, you have secondary letting. That creates a small manageable amount of white space on the page and makes the page look much easier to read. Okay. So let's move on here a little bit. Oh, I would present the product here again after my reason number four. In here, I would show a picture of the product and I would immediately start talking about the offer. In fact, I would start having an action device now on every spread from here on. And an action device would just be a little box on every two page spread that shows a picture of the product, and it has a burst that says a $500 value, just $59, just 59 pounds, and then has pictures of the premiums with a burst on each one saying, you know, a 10-pound value free or whatever the value is with the word free and big bull. And then says order now and has a telephone number to call or directs them to the page of this thing where the response device appears so that they can fill out the response device. So, yeah. And by the way, stuff like this, especially if you're giving away stuff free, it's not an order form. Okay. Don't call it an order form. Guss, heat up a little bit. You know, make it a free gift certificate because we're giving away, as we'll see in a minute, we're going to give them, you know, a couple of books if they buy this thing. So, excuse me, make it a free gift certificate or something else that lends value to it. All right. Here's another problem that is very common on page seven. Now, page seven, you know, the thing is if you start numbering on page one, every odd numbered page is right-hand page. Every left-hand page has an even number on it. Well, this is a right-hand page. This is an odd numbered page. And there's no running text on it at all. The previous page had running text and then points to read stories of and then goes to a full page. So, you know, you can essentially sidebar with three or four testimonials on it. I'll show you the bottom of the page here. And once again, we've got the endless line problem. But anyway, basically what you've done here is you've created a barrier to turning the page. It would be very easy simply to move these kinds of full page sidebars to the left-hand page, have the running text running on the right-hand page, and then at the bottom of that page say please turn. And then you can see that they're used at the bottom of the right-hand page. So, that's just another little graphic technique that helps readership. Remember, every paragraph has to sell the reader on reading the next paragraph, and the bottom of every right-hand page has to sell the reader on turning that page and continuing to read. All right. Let's jump ahead here. Let's see here. Page eight, let's look at one of the subheads that could be improved. No, I don't care what you've tried before. Now, the best subheads are the ones that ask a question, present a benefit, or intrigue the prospect into reading what's beneath it. This does none of those, and really none of this, almost none of the subheads in this do that. So, this is an example of where we could improve readability, and these are little eye hooks. So, we want to grab the prospect and draw him down into the text. Let's skip up to page eleven. This is the credibility of the author, and somebody made a comment about that earlier about the fact that his credibility elements were not up front. I would definitely have references to, you know, you've seen him on the internet, and I think he's a great writer. And about the fact that his credibility elements were not up front. I would definitely have references to, you know, you've seen him on BBC One, you've seen him in the National Press, you know, you've read him in the Daily Telegraph, you know. And now, you know, for the first time, Oliver Grohler shows you how you can blah, blah, blah. And, you know, you can imagine that as a blurb along with that big headline that presented the benefit in the form of a testimonial. I think it would be huge. I wanted to find a testimonial here. All right, well anyway, let's skip ahead. We're running short on time. Let's go to page eleven. Here we go. Yeah. Okay, down in here we're beginning to justify our price. I thought this was interesting. He talks about how he could easily charge, Oliver could easily charge 500 pounds or more for this information. So, you know, he's starting to justify his price, but he hasn't presented his price yet. So here we kind of have an organizational thing. Okay. I would stick with a product description only in this section, focusing on the benefits, all the ways it brings value to your life, how it's step by step, do this, then do that blueprint, making money on eBay, how quickly you can move through and get your business going, how easy it is to do it, an eighth grader could do this. In fact, I would probably go get an eighth grader, make him do it, and then use him as case study in this piece, and demonstrate how fast the money begins flowing. That's a good secondary way to use testimonials, by the way. You know, all of the testimonials, if you've used all of your testimonials in this piece, then I would just go back through and I would take the time period for each one of them. It took me six months to get up to 4,000 pounds a week, and just six months, eight months, two months, one month, two weeks, whatever, the money starts flowing very quickly, and demonstrate that. Anyway, in this area here, we really need to fully dimensionalize the benefits that the product delivers. Also, you know, I really want to see the product. I want to see the discs. This is a 2DVD set, right? So I want to see the DVDs. I want to see the container with the DVDs. I want to see a couple watching a video, watching the DVDs on a big screen TV. I want to see a student working on a computer at home. Okay? I want to see a guy holding a bank statement with a deposit in his bank account from PayPal. I want to see the premiums that you're talking about, and with a burst that says a 10-pound value free or whatever it is. People are visual, and you have a visual medium, and you spend money on a color promotion here. And I think just by getting rid of that, or dramatically condensing the missing ingredient material that was up front, you'll make room to really engage me with emotional photographs that help me visualize myself making a fortune with this thing. I'd also, on page 14, include a sidebar for scanners, and maybe even it would go earlier. I said earlier I wanted to point out these headlines, instead of being in third person, should be in first person in the testimonial giver's voice. Now you need to check your laws. I don't know what the laws are in the UK, but here in the States, they allow us to paraphrase or to edit testimonials. So you may have to take it out of quotes. Again, check the laws here in the UK, but here, this could be you've given me a new lust for life. Put it in the customer's voice so that the reader can see himself saying that, having that experience in saying those things. On page 14, I would add a sidebar that basically shows how simple it is. That basically is simple as one, two, three. You get a product, I'll show you how. You follow the step-by-step instructions I give you. You spend the money. One, two, three. Okay. On page 15, the offer copy is pretty confusing and a little misleading. And I think that's hurting you. It says here I can try this for free, but I really can't. I have to pay 59 pounds to buy the product. If I don't like it, then I can get my money back. So when I read this, I'm thinking, I tried for free. Wow, it's a free trial offer. It must be a soft offer. It must be a bill me. He'll send it to me, let me use it for a month, and then he'll bill me for it. But then as I read it, I say, no, that's not the case at all. So now I'm starting to wonder about your credibility. I'm starting to wonder if you're honest or not, because you told me I could try it for free, but the only way I can get it is to give you 59 dollars or 59 pounds up front. So I think we're too clever by half here. And I would back off on this. Another better way of doing it would be to say, my continual cell system will make you thousands of pounds every month, starting almost immediately, or it's free, or it costs you nothing. Saying it that way, then you can go straight into the explanation of the guarantee. Also, and we need to move on to the next piece here, because we're almost out of time, but I think your closing is a little bit out of order. You should present your price first, then trivialize it, then present your guarantee. Here what we do is we have the guarantee first and then the price. The guarantee is what relieves the risk from the price. So present your price first, this copy. It's only 59 pounds, scream and deal. You'll make more than that your first week, I guarantee it, blah, blah, blah. Then trivialize, 59 pounds. I mean, jeez, how much does it cost to fill up a tank of gas these days? Find some way to trivialize the price or to compare it to 59 pounds for a system that's making a difference. Fred, over here, 4,000 pounds a month. This has got to be the best deal that ever happened. Then add the guarantee. Plus, either I make you money or I don't make money. I wouldn't dream of keeping your money if I'm not making you richer, but just let me know and I'll send you your money back and present your guarantee that way. Finally, there's the idea that crossroads close. I don't have time to get into it now, but I've written about it in the total package. If you want to refresh your memory on that, go ahead to the archives to check it out. The concept there is simple. It's just simply that, hey, look, until now you've been doing it on your own and look what a mess you're in. You're broke. You're failing. Other people are punking you. They're making a lot more money than you are. Including all of the people who've gotten my course. You have two choices. It's totally up to you. You can go on from here the way you've always done it and look where that's gotten you. Or we can go on from here together. I'll give you these proven secrets that will make you a fortune. The final one, and we're going to have to breeze through this one, guys. I'm afraid the company is going to cut us off if I don't cut this a little bit short. The product here is a Chinese herb called Jiao Gulen. Once again, that name needs some serious help. In this case, the name needs help for a different reason. Well, two reasons. The first one is that it's impossible to pronounce for most people. But the main reason is that it commoditizes this product. Now, this is a supplement product, right? And it contains this Jiao Gulen herb, right? But that commoditizes it. That makes it seem like, well, why should I pay you? I could probably go to a Chinese food store if I live in San Francisco and just buy the herb. The product has no mystique. It has no personality. It's just a root or something. It's just part of a plant. So I would definitely work on the name. Come up with a name that has a benefit, has a little bit of intrigue to it. I thought this is a good opportunity to teach this. And I've got a couple of my own copywriters on the phone right now. So look, guys. Notice how the way this is structured, it's very passive. The anti-aging herb, that can extend your life to an active 100 years slammer. You can make this active just by changing this word to this. This anti-aging herb can extend your life to an active 100 years. Now you've got something that's a bold statement. It's a bold proposition. And it warrants a slammer at the end of it, an exclamation point at the end of it. So take a look at your subheads and headlines and ask yourself, what can I do to make them active rather than passive? Now notice that this headline here, though, is about extending my life to an active 100 years. I'm not sure that's what everyone wants. I'm not sure that's even something anyone thinks about. People don't want to die in their sleep tonight. People don't want to have a heart attack and get hauled off to the hospital emergency room today. But to live 100 years, I'm not sure a lot of people sit around and think, wow, I really want to live 100 years. So I think the headline really misses the mark here. Not to mention, we've got major delay gratification here. I was saying, bring it up, bring it to the current time. Bring it to today and show me how you're going to help me right now today. The writer of this piece has a problem, and it's fairly common in the health industry. The problem is that you have a supplement that does a lot of great stuff. We'll see as we get into the copy. This thing does great stuff. And so we end up with a very generalized headline that doesn't pinpoint a particular problem your prospect is dealing with right now. The big advantage you have in the health market is that everyone you're talking to has a body, and every person that you're talking to is experiencing some kind of a problem with that body, whether it's a sexual problem, arthritis, hair loss, wrinkles, high blood pressure, cholesterol, diabetes, the whole shopping list there. And that tends to be what drives people into this market in the first place. So whenever you're faced with a supplement that accomplishes a lot of things, you're going to have to make a Sophie's choice and come up with the one thing that's most likely to resonate with the largest portion of your marketplace. And in this case, I think it would be heart, heart health. But we can, you know, talk about that here in a second. All right, so anyway, that's the top of the page. All right, notice here, this is another little thing. Notice how this is the bottom of page one, and we complete the page with a complete thought. This should be a hanger. There should be a partial sentence or even, you know, the ending with an ellipsis that would, you know, drive you to the next page. Finishing with a punctuation mark on page one is, you know, a mistake that a lot of people make. I thought this was funny. Let's look at page two here. See, now we're getting into some of the things it does that I'm excited about. Diabetes, blood pressure, Alzheimer's cancer. Wow. You know, talking about that kind of stuff on the cover and I'm going to read it. I thought this was kind of cute. And unfortunately, some of your readers may as well, and it'll lose your credibility. The, here we have the researchers going into China and they're noticing that there are people who are living 100 years or more. Okay. So what do they do? After 10 long years of study, they found that a major key to the remarkable health and longevity was the use of an herb called, oh, here it is, Xiao Guilan. Okay. And that it's so effective, this anti-aging property is so effective, Chinese herbalists review it as the immortality or, okay, it took us 10 years to figure out there's something called the immortality or might be somehow connected to the fact that these people live to be 100. Didn't really work for me. I, there's something missing there. I think it's more likely that the researchers went to the city and found out people who live to be 100. They have something they call the immortality or, and the researchers came back and studied it for 10 years to figure out how it worked or if it did. But anyway, it raises more questions than an answer, sir. All right. At this point, I'm reading this thing and there's no credibility element on the cover. Okay. There's, you know, we've got some guy, I don't know, telling me that this thing that he wants to sell me could make me live to be 100. Okay. And it'll do all of these other wonderful things for me. And the same guy that I don't know is telling me he'll, you know, give me, you know, all of this stuff. And this is only about the 20th or 30th promotion for a health product I've gotten in my mailbox this week. And they're all by guys that don't know all telling me that their thing is going to do these fantastic things for me. And, okay. So now I'm on page three and it's still some guy I really don't know. I know now he's an author and a researcher. Okay. But now I'm getting more claims immortality or, okay, list of proven benefits. Okay. Now I'm finally up to here. I guess I'm on page four now. And lookie here. We have another buried lead. We have the, generally the American Medical Association tacitly endorsing this particular product. All members of the two most popular classes of lipid lowering drugs, the fibres and the statins, cause cancer and oh, I don't know. I'm sorry. That's not the one. I have another, oh, Vanderbilt University. It's on page three. Must be at the bottom of the page. Anyway, you'll have to find it. I had it a minute ago. So we have Vanderbilt University saying that this thing works. Okay. That should be on the front cover. You know, Vanderbilt University says. Okay. We also find out here that it's 82% effective in treating hypertension. And as we read on, we'll find out it's 93% effective at lowering cholesterol. And we'll find out that it has a antioxidant cancer connection. It also lowers stress hormones. Does that mean there could be a weight loss claim made because it suppresses cortisol production? Probably. There's a section on the immune system on page five. Let's go up there if we can find it. Okay. Here's where we have the stress. It suppresses stress. It stimulates when it's depressed. It prevents stress hormones from flooding your body. That sounds like it could become a weight loss claim to me because cortisol causes your body to pack on fat. Say goodbye to the cold and flu season. Okay. Here we've got a three-line section on say goodbye to the cold and flu season. The immune system is about a lot more than just the cold and flu season. The immune system keeps cancer cells in check. That plus the antioxidant elements, I think you could have a very strong argument. And if you don't want to use the word cancer, don't use it. Use abnormal cell growth or whatever your legal beagles will let you get away with it. I kind of feel like we're kind of moving through the copy too quickly here. And we are because this is a very short piece. And so, you know, like for example over here where we're talking about insomnia. Okay. Well, I love the fact that insomnia is in here because everybody over the age of 45 experiences it. Right. So that's good. But I think more could be done to talk about, for example, the fact that insomnia has been shown to elevate the risk of heart attack and stroke, fatal accidents, some percentage of early high percentage of all car accidents are caused by people who are sleep deprived. And you're talking here that this is a 95% cure rate for insomnia. And that number is buried down here in the little tiny copy. So I guess my main crit here beyond, you know, some of the others I've already given you is that you've got a product here that's been used for what, thousands of years. Okay. It's been shown, it's been said to be effective by Vanderbilt, which is a very prestigious medical school in this country. And you have citations from other medical colleges in China. You have actual percentages showing how effective this particular supplement is at doing things. You also have a sidebar where doctors are talking about. You've got credibility out the wazoo, but it's buried. And right now in this very highly competitive market, the health supplement market, when you've got credibility like that, you've got to put it right up front. You don't have to put it all on the cover. But you've got a front cover and a back cover to this thing, and you should have a credibility, a major credibility element on each. And when you're talking about how this product, you know, that list of benefits you had on the front cover, when you're talking about things this product does, use the specifics. 95% on insomnia, 82% on hypertension, 93% lowering cholesterol. Now give the numbers medically proven, you know, by many of the world's top or leading medical institutions and clinics. 93% effective at lowering cholesterol, you know, without drug side effects. And for pennies, a dose. You need to look at that too, especially when you're just buying your priceness piece, because, you know, this product costs a heck of a lot less than a lipitor. And it doesn't suppress CoQ10 production in the body and give you heart failure. So, I mean, I think what happened here was that we tried to get this piece done in nine or 10 or 12 pages and went to really to fully romance the product. You needed to tell the entire story, including, you know, all of your credibility elements. So, on page nine also, I would say, don't forget about the company behind the product. Now we've talked, this is a doctor sidebar I mentioned. Don't forget about the company behind the product. As I read through this, I thought, okay, I'm going to get a bunch of Chinese herbs ground up in a, and stuffed into capsules. I got a couple of problems with that. Number one, you know, there have been a lot of stories lately about impure and contaminated products coming out of China and people being poisoned by them. So, you know, and a lot of your prospects are, you know, they're health nuts by definition, right? And so they've read that stuff. They know that. So, you know, you've got to convince me that your company offers the finest form of this product available. And you've got to show me how your, the quality of the initial material is the very finest. How you guarantee the purity of the product. How the dosage in each capsule or tablet is exactly the amount that's been shown to cause the greatest health, positive health effects. You know, show me a picture of the laboratory where you make it with a bunch of people walking around in white coats and hair nets and little baggies on their shoes. You know, give me some testimonials about the, tell me about your company. How long have you been in business? How many people have you helped so far? How fast is the product shipped? You know, how is your guarantee better than the competition? You have testimonials from people say, wow, I ordered on Monday and got it on Thursday. And so now I don't have to think that I'm going to order this and have to wait four to six weeks for delivery. And then finally, you know, I've kind of trashed in this copy. I don't mean to be, there are some good points in it. I just haven't had time today to go through them. But on this page, we've been in a nice two column setting with nice short lines. Now, all of a sudden on this page, we've got these long, long, long, long, very hard to read lines. And I'd also like to suggest that, you know, in the consumer market, you're talking to people who are used to getting their health information largely from magazines and from television. They're not as comfortable. Even the people who are getting subscribing to health newsletters, they subscribe to those health newsletters as a result of a big, fat, all singing, all dancing, tabloid, you know, four color process, 24 pages, or magologue, you know, or something equally colorful. And so when you go with two colors like this, you're kind of speaking to them in a voice that they're not comfortable responding to. And so I would seriously consider longer copy and investing more time and energy in your graphics and definitely going for color. So let's see what else we have on this one. There's some minor things I would probably add. I see how you offered your, structured your offer, and it's very, very similar to one of my clients offer structures. So I suggest, or I suspect you may have seen some of his pieces. Okay, I've got a few questions that we'll handle here before we get off today. Let's see, Ahsoka said on the first piece, having a graphic at George Washington under the title, Thieving Liars Club seems incongruous to me. I totally agree. Also, you know, it kind of looked or felt like, didn't it, that I was being invited to join a club, and then I somehow qualified by being a Thieving Liar myself. It's just part of this, you know, it's hard for me to do sometimes because I'm so close to my copy, but you've got to just step away from it and just take a totally strange look at it. And if you show your copy to 10 people, you're going to get some, you know, some whacked out reactions to your copy. And you'll be tempted to say, ah, nobody would look at it like that. But somebody just did, you know, one of those 10 people that you just showed it to came up with an observation that some percentage of your market will come up with, and this is a game of numbers. You lose one order and you can lose the control if you're a competitive copywriter. You lose one order if you're a business owner and you lose, you know, you can drive your ROI to levels where you can't even promote effectively. So you've got to shave the percentages. Um, see, Derek wanted to know if I recommend a research to writing ratio. No, I don't. Not at all. Every project is different. When I'm approaching one project, I may have to do a day or two of research. I usually insist that my clients provide me with the research because I'm a great, I think I'm a pretty good copywriter, but I'm probably not one of the even top one million researchers in the world, and there are other people that do it better. But when I do have to research, I try to just duck out, get what I need and come back. Quite often I'll, if I don't know a number, for example, and I'm writing about so far this year, the Chinese stock market is up a certain amount. I just put in X percent, let the client fill in the numbers, because when I'm not writing copy, I'm not making money, and when I'm doing research, I'm not making money. So, um, D. Sherrill agreed with me on the name of the product. Steve pointed out that in the second piece, the author buried his credentials. I think we covered that. You're absolutely right, Steve. John wants to know what's the maximum number of lines to have in a three-column paragraph or a two-column paragraph. I think I answered that. Rick Dilema wants to know, and his sales letter has copy stronger, one or three-column format. Okay, in a sales letter is structured to look like a letter to a friend, and so sales letters should be in a typewriter typeface, and it should be one column, and it should look like a letter. Like you just sat down at your old Smith-Corona and typed it out. Right? That's what a sales letter is. If you're doing a special report, if you're doing your promotion in a special report format, now you have some flexibility, and I would not recommend a full page with columns. I would go two-column or three-column. If you're doing a larger piece, a larger page size, such as a tabloid, I've done four column pieces. The ideal, you don't want your line length to be much shorter than 30 or 32 characters, and I don't like my line length being much longer than 30 or 32 characters. I try to keep it in that area, and of course varying the type size can make that happen, regardless of the column width that you're using. Let's see here. John says, do I recommend those vertical lines between columns in the agorapies? Absolutely not. John, you're absolutely right. That's an iPad obstacle, and it just takes up space, and it'll either make the columns look too close to the line, or you'll have to separate the columns, making the line lengths even shorter to accommodate those stupid lines that will not sell one single freaking thing. Some artists just wanted to stick them in there to feel like he was doing a job. So, no, get rid of borders around pages, get rid of lines between columns of text that are pointless. I forget who it was. It said that in direct response, every piece of white space, every graphic element, every word has to earn its place. If it's not helping you sell the product, then it has to go. Tony Osion wants to know, how many different leads do I typically provide to a client before you flesh out the rest of the copy to the lead? I don't do it that way, Tony. What I will typically do is, well, it depends on the client, I guess, but what I'll typically do is I'll read the material, I'll look at the product, and I'll come up with the lead that I want to go with, and then I'll write on that lead. And if the client knew what lead to start with, he could write the thing himself. There are clients that will want to talk to you about the lead, and there are some that will say that they want to see three or four leads, and I just tell them, no, not in that business. I'm not going to throw a whole bunch of stuff mud at the wall at the client, assuming he knows what's going to work. There's only one person whose opinion matters in this whole thing, and it's the market. And the copywriter is being hired because he supposedly has a grasp on what the market's looking for and how that connects with the product in the most powerful way. So I don't believe in democracies when it comes to copywriting. And I have to tell you, if I send a little adamant on that, it's because every time I let my guard down and let a client suggest my lead or my theme or something like that, I end up wasting my time writing the package, like on that KCI piece where they asked me to write about the graying of the baby boomers. So my policy is that I want to immerse myself in the product, immerse myself in the market, read the data cards for the list that they're going to be nailing this to, get to know my prospects pretty well, read the other things that they're reading, by looking at my competitors' pieces and so forth, and then play the part of my prospect, put myself in the prospect's shoes, imagine I'm him, how would I feel? What would be bothering me? What would I be thinking about right now? What would be keeping me from going to sleep at night or waking me up in the middle of the night? And then from there, I would come up with my own lead. So, okay guys, well that's it. I want to thank you all very much for today. Bye-bye.
